Addressing these challenges requires viewing cybersecurity not as a technical IT issue but as a National Security Priority that demands a "Whole-of-Society" response.

2024 - Describe the context and salient features of the Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023. (10)

The DPDP Act, 2023 aims to transform India from a "privacy-neutral" state to a "privacy-centric" digital democracy. It provides the legal backbone for India’s $1 trillion digital economy aspirations.

Context of the Act

1.

2.

  • 3.

  • 4.

  • 5.

  • 6.

7.


Judiciary as a ‘catalyst’ - The landmark Justice K.S. Puttaswamy v. Union of India (2017) judgment

declared privacy a fundamental right under Article 21.

Committee Recommendations (Justice B.N. Srikrishna Committee) emphasizing "Data Sovereignty" and the "Fiduciary" relationship.


Digital economy- With over 900 million internet users, the rapid expansion of digital payments (UPI) and digital public infrastructure (Aadhar, CoWIN) required robust safeguards.

Inadequacy of IT Act, 2000- The previous framework (Section 43A) was narrow, outdated, and lacked the "teeth" to penalize global tech giants for data breaches.

To remain a global outsourcing hub, India needed a law compatible

with Global Norms. Eg- EU’s GDPR.

Data Breaches highlighted the vulnerability of citizens' personal data. Eg- CoWIN data leak

The rise of AI-driven behavioral profiling and "dark patterns" in e-commerce necessitated "Purpose Limitation."

  • 8.    Data has become the "new oil" in modern warfare, with data localization as a vital component of national security.

Salient Features of the Act

  • 1.    The Act is built on the philosophy of "Rightful Processing"

  • 2.    Tripartite Stakeholder Model- Identifies the Data Principal (individual), Data Fiduciary (entity deciding data use), and Data Processor (entity handling data).

  • 3.    Consent-First Approach- Processing is only lawful with "free, specific, informed, unconditional, and unambiguous" consent via a clear notice.

  • 4.    Rights of Data Principals- Grants the right to Access (summary of data), Correction, Erasure, and Nomination (bequeathing digital data after death).

  • 5.    Significant Data Fiduciaries (SDFs)- Entities handling high-volume or sensitive data (e.g., Social Media) must appoint a Data Protection Officer (DPO) and conduct annual audits.

  • 6.    Protection of Minors- Mandates verifiable parental consent for children (under 18) and strictly prohibits tracking or targeted advertising directed at them.

  • 7.    Data Protection Board of India (DPBI)- A digital-first regulatory body empowered to investigate breaches and impose fines.

  • 8.    Negative List for Cross-Border Flow- Permits data transfer to most countries unless specifically restricted by a government "Blocklist."

  • 9.    Stringent Financial Penalties- Forgoes criminal jail terms in favor of massive civil penalties-up to ₹250 crore for failure to prevent a data breach.

Challenges That Remain

  • 1.    Surveillance concerns- Section 17 allows the state to bypass most provisions for "security of the state" and "public order".

  • 2.    Diligence vs. Innovation- high cost of implementing "Privacy by Design" and maintaining audit trails for MSMEs and startups.

  • 3.    One-size-fits-all approach- Unlike GDPR, the Indian law does not distinguish between general data and "Sensitive" data.

The Act is a right step toward Digital Sovereignty. It must move beyond mere legal text to create a "Privacy Culture" for meaningful exercise of digital autonomy.

2018 - Data security has assumed significant importance in the digitized world due to rising cyber-crimes. Justice B. N. Srikrishna Committee Report addresses issues related to data security. What, in your view, are the strengths and weaknesses of the Report relating to protection of personal data in cyber space? (15)

The Justice B.N. Srikrishna Committee's 2018 report - "A Free and Fair Digital Economy" - was India's foundational attempt to translate the privacy jurisprudence of Puttaswamy (2017) into statutory architecture. The Rising Scale of Cybercrime in India

  • 1.    Cybercrime cases rose from 10.29 lakh in 2022 to 28.15 lakh in 2025 (MHA, I4C).

  • 2.    Indians lost approximately ₹22,495 crore in 2025

  • 3.    Cybercrime complaints have grown by over 623% between 2021 and 2024 on the NCRP portal.

  • 4.    77% of fraud losses stem from investment scams; digital arrests (9%) and sextortion (4%) are the fastest-growing categories (I4C, 2025).

  • 5.    I4C has frozen 24.67 lakh mule accounts and blocked 9.42 lakh SIM cards linked to cyber fraud

Key Recommendations of the Srikrishna Committee Report

  • 1.    Citizen reframed as data principal; entity as data fiduciary with trust obligations.

  • 2.    Enshrined principles of consent, purpose limitation, data minimisation, and storage limitation.

  • 3.    Heightened protection for sensitive personal data - health, biometric, financial, religious, genetic

  • 4.    Created an independent Data Protection Authority (DPA) with adjudicatory powers.

  • 5.    Mandated data localisation for critical and sensitive personal data within India.

  • 6.    Recognised new-age rights - confirmation, correction, portability, right to be forgotten.

  • 7.    Special safeguards for children's data, including parental consent and a ban on profiling.

  • 8.    Cross-border transfer permitted only via adequacy mechanisms or contractual safeguards.

Strengths of the Report

  • 1.    Constitutional anchoring in Puttaswamy - privacy treated as a fundamental right, not a regulatory courtesy.

  • 2.    Fiduciary framing imposes a trust-based duty on data handlers, drawing from common law traditions.

  • 3.    GDPR-aligned principles of purpose limitation and accountability bring India to global standards.

  • 4.    Independent regulator (DPA) institutionalises enforcement beyond executive discretion.

  • 5.    Empowerment of citizens through actionable rights - correction, portability, erasure.

  • 6.    Sectoral sensitivity through layered protection for health, financial, biometric, and children's data.

  • 7.    Digital sovereignty advanced through data localisation provisions for critical data.

  • 8.    Balanced approach - does not stifle innovation; permits research, journalism, and reasonable business processing.

Weaknesses of the Report

  • 1.    Broad State exemptions - surveillance under Section 35 permits processing in the interest of national security, public order, etc., without prior judicial oversight.

  • 2.    Weak independence of the DPA - appointment process dominated by the executive raises capture concerns.

  • 3.    Ambiguity on "critical" personal data - left to executive notification, creating regulatory uncertainty.

  • 4.    Data localisation costs disproportionately affect MSMEs, startups, and global service providers.

  • 5.    No clear remedy framework for data breaches - compensation provisions remain vague and non-deterrent.

  • 6.    Inadequate provisions on non-personal data - a critical gap addressed only later by the Kris Gopalakrishnan Committee.

  • 7.    No special framework for emerging threats - AI profiling, deepfakes, biometric coercion are underaddressed.

  • 8.    Limited attention to journalistic and whistleblower data, raising press-freedom concerns.

Way Forward

  • 1.    Operationalise the Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023, with timely framing of subordinate rules.

  • 2.    Establish the Data Protection Board of India (DPBI) as a genuinely independent authority, on the lines of TRAI or SEBI.

  • 3.    Bring State surveillance under judicial pre-authorisation, in line with the Puttaswamy proportionality test.

  • 4.    Strengthen CERT-In, I4C, and NCIIPC capacities with sustained funding and inter-agency coordination.

  • 5.    Mandate algorithmic transparency and AI-impact assessments for high-risk processing.

  • 6.    Operationalise the Cyber Fraud Mitigation Centre (CFMC) to scale real-time fraud interception.

  • 7.    Launch a national digital literacy mission focusing on Tier-2/3 cities, senior citizens, and first-time users.

  • 8.    Promote international cooperation through Budapest Convention engagement and bilateral data-sharing protocols.

"Data is the new oil." Thus, protection of personal data is no longer a technological concern but a constitutional one.

2019 - What is CyberDome Project? Explain how it can be useful in controlling internet crimes in India. (10)

The CyberDome Project is a high-tech PPP initiative of Kerala Police, established as a "Centre of Excellence" to combat emerging cyber threats through collaborative research and development.

Rising Internet Crimes in India

  • 1.    Cybersecurity incidents rose from 10.29 lakh in 2022 to 22.68 lakh in 2024 (120% increase in two years)

  • 2.    Massive Financial Toll- over ₹36.45 lakh crore as per NCRP.

  • 3.    Significant rise in AI-driven phishing and "Digital Arrest" scams

  • 4.    Enforcement agencies have blocked over 9.42 lakh SIM cards and 2.63 lakh IMEIs linked to fraudulent activities by early 2025.

  • 5.    Over 86% of households are now connected to the internet - High vulnerability

  • 6.    13.7% of global incidents target India (Cyfirma report)

Importance of CyberDome in Controlling Internet Crimes

  • 1.    Shift from "reactive investigation" approach to "proactive defense" model

  • 2.    Public-Private Collaboration bridges the talent gap by involving over 2,500 volunteers, including ethical hackers and IT experts

  • 3.    Real-Time Threat Intelligence using AI and machine learning. In 2024, it successfully thwarted a major DDoS attack on an Indian financial institution.

  • 4.    Combatting Online Exploitation through initiatives like "Hac’KP 2025" and the "KID GLOVE" program (international recognition from INTERPOL)

  • 5.    In September 2024, it launched a Security Operation Centre (SOC) to monitor police networks and prevent sensitive data breaches.

  • 6.    Specialized Cyber-Wings- It operates niche units like the "Ransomware School" and a Malware Analysis Lab, creating Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs).

  • 7.    Academic Synergy- Collaborations with institutions like NIT Calicut (2024 MoU)

  • 8.    Financial Fraud Mitigation detected vulnerabilities in 4 out of 10 banking apps tested (CAG report)

  • 9.    Officers and volunteers act as "Online Police Patrols," monitoring social media for extremist propaganda, radicalization efforts, and the spread of fake news.

The CyberDome Project has transformed the Kerala Police into a tech-forward force, achieving a 25% increase in case resolution between 2022 and 2024.

2024 - Social media and encrypting messaging services pose a serious security challenge. What measures have been adopted at various levels to address the security implications of social media? Also suggest any other remedies to address the problem. (15)

India has second-largest social media market with 491 million active users. However, in 2025, these platforms are central to "Grey Zone Warfare" as their architecture creates a "security paradox"

Challenges Posed by Social Media & Encrypted Messaging

Social Media Challenges

  • 1.    AI-Generated deepfakes and synthetic media. Eg-doctored videos during local elections.


  • 2.    Algorithmic Echo Chambers creating polarized "digital silos". (India Hate Lab report)

  • 3.    Cognitive Warfare- Eg- "Bot-farms" to build anti-India narratives

  • 4.    Cyber Stalking and Harassment - Eg- Women targeted through doxxing and revenge porn.

  • 5.    Online Radicalisation - Eg- ISIS recruitment through social media.

Encrypted Messaging Challenges

  • 1.    Traceability Paradox- End-to-end encryption prevents law enforcement from identifying the "first

originator"

  • 2.    Operational Backbone for Terror- Eg- ISIS and LeT using Telegram or Signal

  • 3.    Serve as the front-end for darknet markets dealing in narcotics and illegal firearms.

  • 4.    Shadow Financial Hubs- Eg- use of "self-destructing" messages for "Digital Arrest" extortions

Measures Adopted at Various Levels

Legal and Regulatory Framework

  • 1.    IT Rules, 2021 - mandate Traceability, requiring significant social media intermediaries to identify the

"first originator" on court orders.

  • 2.    DPDP Act- Requires data breach notifications to the Data Protection Board within 72 hours.

  • 3.    Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita Section 353 penalizes the spread of misinformation causing public fear.

Institutional and Operational Measures

  • 1.    I4C acts as the national nodal point for identifying and removing unlawful content.

  • 2.    CyberDome Project (Kerala) to monitor the Darknet and social media for criminal patterns.

  • 3.    "Operation Blackface" of Maharashtra Police Cyber Department for crack down on Child Sexual Abuse Material (CSAM)

Global and Social Initiatives

  • 1.    India actively participates in the UN Convention against Cybercrime (2024) and the Christchurch Call to eliminate extremist content.

  • 2.    Initiatives like Cyber Swachhta Kendra and the 1930 Helpline promote "Digital Hygiene."

Suggested Remedies to Address the Problem

  • 1.    Metadata Analysis over Decryption- By analyzing "who talks to whom, when, and from where" (metadata), to identify criminal clusters.

  • 2.    Mandating social media platforms to undergo independent audits of their "Recommendation Engines."

  • 3.    Implementing mandatory Digital Watermarking for all AI-generated content (deepfakes).

  • 4.    Establishing an independent, statutory Digital Ombudsman to provide a "Single-Window" grievance redressal mechanism for citizens

  • 5.    Mandatory SIM-Binding for messaging apps to prevent the use of international virtual numbers in local scams like "Digital Arrests."

  • 6.    Integrating "Cognitive Defense" and digital civics into school and college curricula.

  • 7.    Formalizing a real-time data-sharing bridge between the I4C and tech giants to instantly "freeze" fraudulent financial flows during the Golden Hour.

  • 8.    Community Fact-Checking Models- Empowering certified organizations with "Priority Reporting" status on platforms to flag virally dangerous misinformationce.

  • 9.    Push for a Global Cyber-Extradition Framework to ensure that criminals operating from "Safe Haven" countries can be prosecuted.

  • 10.    Sovereign Messaging Alternatives- Eg- apps like Sandes for government communication.

  • 11.    Adopting global best practices

  • a.    "Duty of Care" Principle (UK)- Shifting burden of safety from the user to the platform.

  • b.    EU’s Digital Services Act for Algorithmic Transparency

  • c.    Estonia’s e-residency program using Blockchain

For "Safe and Trusted Cyberspace" there is need for a "Whole-of-Nation" approach

Border Management and Security Forces

2019 - Cross-Border movement of insurgents is only one of the several security challenges facing the policing of the border in North-East India. Examine the various challenges currently emanating across the India-Myanmar border. Also, discuss the steps to counter the challenges. (15)

The instability following the 2021 Myanmar military coup has transformed 1,643 km India-Myanmar border into a ‘high-risk zone’

Security Challenges Emanating Across the India-Myanmar Border

  • 1.

  • 2.

  • 3.

  • 4.

  • 5.

  • 6.

  • 7.

  • 8.

  • 9.


“Safe Havens” for insurgents like NSCN-K or ULFA-I

Parallel government by insurgent groups. Eg- charging 1% to 2% "revolutionary tax" on legal infrastructure projects and "safe passage fees" from drug mafias

Refugee Crisis & Demographic Shifts- Since the 2021 coup, over

95,000 refugees (primarily Chin and Sagaing residents) have entered

Mizoram and Manipur.

"The Golden Triangle" Spillover- Myanmar’s civil war has led to a surge in poppy cultivation. Profits are used to fund ethnic militias, creating a "narcotics-insurgency nexus."

Arms Smuggling- The border town of Moreh (Manipur) has become a primary transit hub for weaponry smuggled from China.                 L

With the Myanmar military (Tatmadaw) losing control of nearly 86% of its territory, rebel groups like the Chin National Army (CNA) and PDFs operate freely in "ungoverned spaces"

China’s deep investments in Myanmar (CMEC) creates threat to India’s "Chicken’s Neck" corridor Misuse of FMR- Although the government scraped it in 2024, enforcing the new "Regulated Border Pass System" remains a challenge due to local opposition.

Overburdened security forces - The Assam Rifles is dual-tasked with border guarding and counter-insurgency.

Steps Taken to Counter the Challenges

  • 1.    Shift to "Security-First" border management strategy

  • 1.    Abolition of FMR (visa-free travel up to 16 km) in 2024

  • 2.    Push for the completion of the Kaladan Multi-Modal Project and the Trilateral Highway by 2027

  • 3.    Operation Sunrise - Joint operation by Indian and Myanmar Army against insurgent groups

  • 4.    Biometric Documentation of all refugees and border-crossers

  • 5.    15 BSF battalions deployed to aid Assam Rifles.

Steps Required to Counter the Challenges

1.    Strict Implementation of the New Pass System

  • 2.    Federal coordination through North-East zonal council

  • 3.    Accelerate the Smart Fencing System (SFS) using laser walls and sensors.

  • 4.    Creation of strictly monitored 10 km "Buffer Zone" to curb spontaneous illegal crossings.

  • 5.    Intelligence Fusion- Set up real-time data-sharing hubs to track the movement of "Golden Triangle" narcotics and arms shipments.

  • 6.    Humanitarian Infrastructure- Build refugee camps near the border to prevent undocumented migrants from blending into local populations.

  • 7.    Engage with local Ethnic Armed Organizations (EAOs) in Myanmar that control the border to secure Indian infrastructure projects.

  • 8.    Extend Vibrant Villages Programme to the eastern border for better infrastructure

  • 9.    Upgrade Outposts (BOPs) with drones and satellite imagery for 24/7 surveillance of forested infiltration routes.

"3C Strategy"of Containment, Cooperation and Connectivity is need to transform the region from a "conflict

zone" to an "economic corridor."

2020 - For effective border area management, discuss the steps required to be taken to deny local support to militants and also suggest ways to manage favourable perception among locals. (10)

Border Management involves securing a nation’s frontiers against unauthorized movement of people and goods while facilitating legitimate trade and commerce.

Steps to Deny Local Support to Militants

  • 1.    Neutralizing Overground Workers to prevent "3L” support to terrorists- Logistics, Location (shelter), and Liaison (intelligence)

  • 2.    Strengthening the Multi-Agency Centre (MAC) to share real-time data between central and state forces to identify local sympathizers.

  • 3.    Curbing Online Radicalization- Monitoring Telegram and social media pages used for indoctrination.

  • 4.    Strict Enforcement of UAPA to create a high legal cost for aiding terrorists.

  • 5.    Monitoring NGOs and Hawala channels that funnel foreign funds to border areas.

  • 6.    Community Policing- Eg- Project Prahari in Assam.

  • 7.    Counter-Propaganda- Using local radio and influencers to highlight the personal costs of supporting violence.

  • 8.    Surrender & Rehabilitation Policy to mainstream misguided youth. Eg- 2024 Revised Surrender Policy in J&K.

Ways to manage favourable perception among locals

  • 1.    Army-led goodwill initiatives - Eg- Army Goodwill Schools under Operation Sadbhavana.

  • 2.    Skill development, entrepreneurship push - Eg- Mission Youth and Himayat

  • 3.    Social infrastructure development under Operation Sadbhavanaibrant Village program

  • 4.    Connectivity initiatives for tourism promotion. Eg- Chenab Bridge, and running Vande Bharat trains

  • 5.    De-radicalisation Centres - Counselling sessions for first-time offenders.

  • 6.    Targeted Security Approach - Eg- Focus on OGWs and hybrid militants without mass crackdowns.

  • 7.    Grassroots Democracy- Empowering PRIs to give locals a say in development.

  • 8.    "Bharat Darshan" Tours- Sponsoring trips for border youth to major Indian cities to foster a sense of national integration.

Building cooperation and trust ensures the population becomes the "First Line of Defence"

2020 - Analyse internal security threats and transborder crimes along Myanmar, Bangladesh and Pakistan borders including Line of Control (LoC). Also discuss the role played by various security forces in this regard. (15)

With 15,106.7 km of land borders, the sanctity of India's frontiers is the primary shield against unconventional "hybrid" warfare and transnational organized crime.

Myanmar Border (1,643 km)

  • 1.    Refugee Influx & Demographic Strain- Post-2021 coup, over 95,000 refugees (Chin and Sagaing residents) have entered Mizoram and Manipur.


  • 2.    Safe Havens for Insurgent Groups (IIGs)- Groups like ULFA-I and NSCN-K utilize "ungoverned spaces" in Myanmar to train and retreat.

  • 3.    "Golden Triangle" Narcotics- Myanmar is a global hub for Methamphetamines. Eg- ED raids in Champhai (Mizoram) uncovering a ₹52.8 crore narco-hawala network.

  • 4.    Precursor Chemical Smuggling- Sending pseudoephedrine from India to Myanmar for drug production.

  • 5.    Weapon Proliferation- Smuggling of sophisticated Western and Chinese arms from Myanmar's black markets into Manipur.

Bangladesh Border (4,096 km)

  • 1.    Illegal Migration- Movement of undocumented individuals

  • 2.    Illegal Cattle Smuggling (billion-dollar) - leads to border skirmishes.

  • 3.    Bangladesh acts as a transit point for high-quality Fake Indian Currency Notes (FICN).

  • 4.    Human Trafficking- A major route for trafficking women and children for forced labor.

  • 5.    Radicalization Spillover- groups like Ansarullah Bangla Team (ABT) attempting to establish sleeper cells in West Bengal and Assam.

Pakistan Border & LoC- The "Hybrid Warfare" Zone (3,323 km)

1.    Drone-Based Narco-Terrorism in Punjab through Golden Crescent
2.    Infiltration of "Hybrid" Terrorists- Eg- The April 2025 Pahalgam attack which killed 26 tourists.

  • 3.    Loitering Munition Attacks to target military infrastructure dueing ‘Op Sindoor’

  • 4.    Role of OGW - generate pro-terror content, circulate videos and organise protests to delegitimise the state.

Role Played by Various Security Forces

India follows the "One Border, One Force" principle to ensure specialized guarding.

Indian Army (Line of Control & Siachen)

  • •    Mandate- High-intensity combat and maintaining the sanctity of the LoC and LAC.

  • •    Counter-Infiltration through Anti-Infiltration Obstacle System (AIOS) (a multi-layered fence equipped with sensors)

  • •    Siachen Defense- Guards the world’s highest battlefield at heights above 20,000 feet.

Border Security Force (BSF)

  • •    Guards the International Borders (IB) with Pakistan and Bangladesh.

  • •    Anti-Smuggling Operations- Intercepts narcotics, cattle, and arms smuggling.

  • •    Implements the Comprehensive Integrated Border Management System (CIBMS).

  • •    Anti-Drone Warfare- Operates specialized jammers and "hard-kill" interceptors

Indo-Tibetan Border Police (ITBP)

  • •    Guards the Indo-China Border (LAC) from Ladakh to Arunachal Pradesh.

  • •    Acts as the first responder for avalanches and landslides in the Himalayan region.

Assam Rifles (AR)

  • •    Guards the Indo-Myanmar Border and conducts counter-insurgency operations in N-E.

  • •    Responsible for implementing the 2024-25 scrapping of the Free Movement Regime (FMR) and biometric profiling of border-crossers.

Sashastra Seema Bal (SSB)

•    Guards the Nepal and Bhutan Borders.

  • •    Acts as the Lead Intelligence Agency (LIA) to identify "Third-Country Nationals"

Other Steps taken

  • 1.    Multi-Agency Centre (MAC) for intelligence sharing

  • 2.    Vibrant Villages Programme

The integration of modern technology with civil-military cooperation is essential to create "Multi-layered Security Shield."

2024 - India has a long and troubled border with China and Pakistan fraught with contentious issues. Examine the conflicting issues and security challenges along the border. Also give out the development being undertaken in these areas under the Border Area Development Programme (BADP) and Border Infrastructure and Management (BIM) Scheme. 15

India’s borders with China (3,488 km) and Pakistan (3,323 km) are among the most militarized and volatile in world. It is marred by territorial disputes and a transition toward high-tech "hybrid warfare"

Conflicting Issues and Security Challenges

India-China Border (Line of Actual Control - LAC)

  • 1.    Disagreement over the "Line of Actual Control" in the Western (Aksai Chin), Middle, and Eastern (Arunachal Pradesh) sectors. China continues to claim Arunachal Pradesh as "South Tibet."

  • 2.    Strategic Encirclement- CPEC passing through

Pakistan-occupied Kashmir (PoK) is seen by India as a violation of sovereignty.

  • 3.    High-Altitude Standoffs- heavy troop deployments post 2020 Galwan clash. Despite a disengagement agreement in October 2024, mutual distrust remains high.

  • 4.    China’s rapid construction of "Xiaokang" (dual-use model villages) and airfields near the LAC puts tactical pressure on c^ n                   xJ

Indian defenses.

India-Pakistan Border (International Border & Line of Control - LoC)

  • 1.    State-Sponsored Terrorism- Continued support for militant groups like LeT and JeM.

  • 2.    Unresolved status of Jammu & Kashmir and frequent ceasefire violations along the LoC

  • 3.    Recent Military Escalation- Following the Pahalgam attack, India launched "Operation Sindoor"

  • 4.    Drone-Based Narco-Terrorism in Punjab through Golden Crescent

  • 5.    Role of OGW - generate pro-terror content, circulate videos and organise protests to delegitimise the state.

  • 6.    Sir Creek Issue remains unresolved

Development Initiatives- BADP and BIM Schemes ("Development-as-Defense" strategy)

Border Area Development Programme (BADP)

  • 1.    Centrally Sponsored Scheme to meet the special developmental needs of people living within 0–50 km of the International Border.

  • 2.    "Model Villages" with socio-economic infrastructure

  • 3.    Infrastructure Development- Eg- Construction of link roads, bridges, and PHCs

  • 4.    Livelihood & Skill Development- vocational training in hospitality, agriculture, and handicrafts

  • 5.    Convergence with the Vibrant Villages Programme (VVP).

  • 6.    Promotion of Border Tourism- Develops "Border Haats" and tourism circuits

  • 7.    Digital Connectivity- Implementation of e-Chaupals and Wi-Fi hotspots

Border Infrastructure and Management (BIM) Scheme

  • 1.    For the period 2021-22 to 2025-26 with an outlay of ₹13,020 crore.

  • 2.    Central Sector Scheme (100% funding by MHA) focused on "Hard Security Infrastructure" and technological modernization.

  • 3.    Smart Fencing (CIBMS)- uses laser walls, thermal imagers, and unattended ground sensors to detect intrusions.

  • 4.    All-weather Connectivity- Construction of strategic roads and tunnels by the Border Roads Organization (BRO). Eg- Sela Tunnel and Shinku La Tunnel.

  • 5.    Frontier Highways- Eg- 1,800 km Highway in Arunachal Pradesh

  • 6.    Creation of new Border Outposts (BOPs) and Company Operating Bases (COBs) with advanced "hard-kill" anti-drone systems.

The integration of modern technology with civil-military cooperation is essential to create "Multi-layered

Security Shield."

CHINA

TIBET

Eastern Sector (1325 Kms exd. Bhutan)

DISPUTED AREAS

* Western Seder

f (1570 Kms)


2022 - What are the maritime security challenges in India ? Discuss the organisational,

technical and procedural initiatives taken to improve maritime security. (10)

11,098 km coastline, an Exclusive Economic Zone of over 2 million sq km, and over 95% of its trade by volume via sea, highlights the need for robust maritime security architecture

Maritime Security Challenges in India

  • 1.    The Indian ocean region is ‘New Hotbed’ of security threats -Fareed Zakaria


  • 2.    Maritime Terrorism- Eg- the 26/11 Mumbai attacks

  • 3.    Geopolitical Competition from China challenging India's role as a "Net Security Provider."

  • a.    "dual-use" ports like Hambantota (Sri Lanka) and Gwadar (Pakistan)

  • b.    Chinese spy ships in Indian Ocean (Tianwen I)

  • 4.    Piracy and Armed Robbery-

  • a.    Somali piracy in the Gulf of Aden and Arabian Sea

  • b.    Houthi disruptions in the Red Sea

  • 5.    Transnational Organized Crime- The "Golden Crescent" and "Golden Triangle" routes converge in the IOR. Drugs worth over ₹11000 crore were seized from seaports in last 5 years. (MHA)

  • 6.    Human Trafficking- Illegal migration routes across the Bay of Bengal and Andaman Sea.

  • 7.    Illegal, Unreported, and Unregulated (IUU) Fishing- Eg- Large foreign deep-sea trawlers encroaching on India’s.

  • 8.    Offshore Asset Vulnerability- Eg- Mumbai High oil rigs and the Great Nicobar Project from state and non-state sabotage.

  • 9.    Nuclearization of IOR due to AUKUS

  • 10.    Rising sea levels and the increasing frequency of "Super Cyclones" threaten critical coastal infrastructure. Eg- naval bases and nuclear plants.

  • 11.    IOR emerging as theater of great-power rivalry - Eg- US-UK base in Diego Garcia

Initiatives to Improve Maritime Security

Organisational Initiatives

  • •    Bureau of Port Security (BoPS) for unified regulatory oversight for port and ship security

  • •    National Maritime Security Coordinator (NMSC) to improve coordination between the Navy, Coast Guard, and various ministries.

  • •    Information Fusion Centre - Indian Ocean Region (IFC-IOR) as a global hub for real-time information sharing on "White Shipping."

  • •    Three-Tiered Security Architecture- *

  • a.    Indian Navy- Deep-sea and International Maritime Boundary Line (IMBL).

  • b.    Indian Coast Guard (ICG)- Intermediate waters and EEZ (up to 200 nm).

  • c.    Marine Police- Coastal waters (up to 12 nm).

Technical Initiatives

•    Coastal Surveillance Network (CSN) Phase-II- Completion of a chain of 46 static radar stations and AIS (Automatic Identification System).

  • •    Mandatory installation of NavIC-enabled Vessel Monitoring System (VMS) on all fishing vessels.

  • •    Induction of MQ-9B SeaGuardian drones for surveillance of sensitive chokepoints.

  • •    Utilization of ISRO's GSAT-7 series (Rukmini) for "dark vessel" tracking.

  • •    Project 75I - for more indigenous scorpian class submarine

Procedural Initiatives

  • •    Indian Maritime Doctrine 2025- formally recognizes "No-War, No-Peace" as a distinct operational category, emphasizing readiness for grey-zone conflicts.

  • •    MAHASAGAR (2025)- fostering security partnerships with IOR littoral states.

  • •    Exercise Sea Vigil- to test the "SOPs" of over 15 different agencies.

  • •    Issuance of biometric ID cards to over 300,000 fishermen.

A proactive Maritime Domain Awareness is needed for resilient "Blue Economy" and “maritime surakshit Bharat”.

2023 - The use of unmanned aerial vehicles(UAVs) by our adversaries across the borders to ferry arms/ammunition, drugs, etc., is a serious threat to internal security. Comment on the measures being taken to tackle this threat. (10)

UAVs have emerged as "aerial dimension" of asymmetric warfare along Indian Maritime and Land Borders. Threat of UAVs to Internal Security

  • 1.    Drones offer a low-cost, high-reward, and low-risk method. Eg- Ukraine used $2,000 drones to disable Russia’s S-300 air defence systems.


  • 2.    Narco-Terror Financing- Drones ferry heroin and "ICE" to fund cross-border insurgencies.

  • 3.    Small Arms Proliferation- Quadcopters drop Turkish-made pistols and grenades for sleeper cells.

  • 4.    Explosive-laden drones target high-value military assets. Eg- 2021 Jammu Air Force Station attack

  • 5.    Radicalization via Propaganda- Drops of subversive literature and inflammatory digital devices in border districts. Eg- Materials recovered in J&K (2025).

  • 6.    Intelligence and Reconnaissance- map border outposts, fence gaps, and troop movement

  • 7.    Swarm Overload Tactics- Deploying multiple UAVs to saturate and confuse conventional radar systems. Eg- "Operation Sindoor" .

  • 8.    Smugglers use "dark vessels" (drones with lights/transponders off) that switch frequencies to evade standard radio-frequency (RF) scanners.

  • 9.    Small DJI-type drones fly below 100 feet at night, exploiting the "radar gap" where traditional air defense is ineffective.

Measures Taken to Tackle the Threat

  • 1.    Mission Sudarshan Chakra - AI-driven air-defense shield for major cities and critical border belts.

  • 2.    Drone Rules, 2021 - For regulating civilian drone rules. Eg- strict import norms

  • 3.    Indrajaal - AI-powered Anti-Drone Patrol Vehicle t0 detect and neutralize drones within 10 km.

  • 4.    Anti-Drone Grid (ARDS) along the Punjab border featuring "Soft Kill" (jammers/spoofers) and "Hard Kill" (lasers/kinetic interceptors).

  • 5.    DRDO-developed D-4 systems and Bhargavastra - use lasers to destroy targets within 2 km.

  • 6.    Integration of thermal imagers and radar into the CIBMS.

  • 7.    Drone Forensics Centers to trace flight paths, launch points, and "Digital Signatures" of captured foreign UAVs.

  • 8.    Indigenous "Kamikaze" "Zombee" Drones designed to crash into hostile drones mid-air.

  • 9.    BSF and State Police have set up mobile checkpoints 5–10 km inside the border to intercept ground-level "receivers."

  • 10.    Community Rewards Scheme to report drone sounds (contributes to 40% of recoveries)

  • 11.    Aatmanirbhar Defense Push- Funding startups via iDEX

  • 12.    Baaj Akh (2025)- Punjab government’s anti-drone system as a second line of defense behind BSF

  • 13.    Role of private sector

  • a.    Nagastra - Loitering munition (suicide drones)

  • b.    Bhargavastra - Counter drone system for swarm detection

A comprehensive National Security Doctrine covering anti-drone measures is needed to ensure “secure skies”

2023 - What are the internal security challenges being faced by India? Give out the role of Central Intelligence and Investigative Agencies tasked to counter such threats. (15)

India’s internal security landscape has become increasingly complex, characterized by the convergence of traditional threats and high-tech "Grey Zone" challenges.

Traditional Internal Security Challenges

1.    Cross-Border Terrorism- Eg- The 2025 Pahalgam attack by the TRF (a proxy of LeT).

  • 2.    Left-Wing Extremism (LWE)- The "Red Corridor" remains a threat in core districts of Chhattisgarh and Odisha.

    3.

    4.

    5.

    6.


    7.

    8.


Insurgency in the North-East- Ethnic militancy and secessionist movements like NSCN (IM).

Communal and Sectarian Violence- Eg- Delhi Riots, 2020

Illegal Migration & Infiltration along Bangladesh and Myanmar borders. Eg- Rohingya

Organized Crime Syndicates-

  • a.    Extortion and smuggling networks. Eg- D-Company

  • b.    Drug Trafficking from Golden Crescent and Golden Traingle

Hawala & Fake Currency (FICN)- Illegal financial channels used to destabilize the economy.

Coastal Security Vulnerabilities

  • a.    sea-borne infiltration. Eg- 26/11 attacks

  • b.    Piracy


  • c.    Illegal deep sea fishing

Emerging Internal Security Challenges

  • 1.    Drone-Enabled Smuggling- Use of UAVs for drug and weapon drops.

  • 2.    Cyber-Warfare-

  • a.    Rise in “Digital arrest” cases

  • b.    13.7% of global incidents target India (Cyfirma report)

  • 3.    Deepfakes & AI-Disinformation- Eg- Manipulated videos during the 2025 state elections.

  • 4.    Online Radicalization and "white-collar" terrorism. Eg- 2025 "Doctor Module" of Red Fort Blast.

  • 5.    Cryptocurrency for Terror Funding- Use of "Privacy Coins" to bypass banking regulators.

Role of agencies

Intelligence Agencies

1.    Intelligence Bureau (IB)-

  • a.    India’s premier internal intelligence agency

  • b.    Manages the Multi-Agency Centre (MAC) to coordinate real-time terror alerts

  • 2.    Research & Analysis Wing (R&AW)- External intelligence agency. Monitors cross-border launch pads, foreign political developments, and global terror financing.

  • 3.    National Technical Research Organisation (NTRO)-

  • a.    The apex body for technical intelligence

  • b.    Manages spy satellites, UAVs, and "Cyber-Surveillance".

  • 4.    Defence Intelligence Agency (DIA)- Integrates intelligence from the Army, Navy, and Air Force

  • 5.    Financial Intelligence Unit-India (FIU-IND)- receive and analyze reports on suspicious financial transactions to track money laundering and terror funding.

  • 6.    Directorate of Revenue Intelligence (DRI)- India’s premier anti-smuggling agency. Monitors trafficking of gold, firearms, and counterfeit currency (FICN). Eg- Operation Molten Metal to bust gold smuggling racket

Investigative Agencies

1.    National Investigation Agency (NIA)-

  • a.    The federal counter-terrorism agency

  • b.    Has Suo Motu powers to investigate terror cases without state consent.

  • c.    Eg- "Operation Octopus" Against PFI

  • 2.    Central Bureau of Investigation (CBI)-

  • a.    Probes high-profile corruption, economic offenses, and serious inter-state crimes

  • b.    Acts as the nodal agency for Interpol in India.

  • 3.    Enforcement Directorate (ED)- enforce the PMLA

  • 4.    Narcotics Control Bureau (NCB)- Apex coordinating body for the drug trade

  • 5.    Serious Fraud Investigation Office (SFIO)- investigate complex white-collar crimes and large-scale corporate frauds.

  • 6.    Wildlife Crime Control Bureau (WCCB)- curbing organized wildlife crime and illegal trade in endangered species.

Advanced intelligence, investigative precision, and grassroots socio-economic development need to work in tandem to safeguard the nation’s democratic fabric.

2025 - Why is maritime security vital to protect India’s sea trade? Discuss maritime and coastal security challenges and the way forward. (15)

Maritime security is the "silent guardian" of India’s economic prosperity. The safety of Sea Lanes of Communication (SLOCs) is not just a military concern but a vital economic imperative.

Importance of maritime security for India’s sea trade

  • 1.    Economic Lifeline- Approximately 95% of India’s trade by volume and 70% by value is conducted via the sea.

  • 2.    Energy Security- India imports over 88% of its crude oil and 51% of its natural gas from chokepoints like the Strait of Hormuz and Malacca Strait.

  • 3.    Global Hub Aspirations- Secure seas are essential for India to emerge as a global transshipment hub

  • 4.    Protection of "Blue Economy"- to protect fisheries, offshore oil/gas (Mumbai High), and seabed minerals in India’s 2.3 million sq. km. Exclusive Economic Zone (EEZ)

  • 5.    Strategic Connectivity- Initiatives like Sagarmala and IMEC rely on secure seas.

  • 6.    Submarine Cable Safety- Almost 99% of India’s internet data travels through undersea fiber-optic cables.

  • 7.    Regional Leadership under SAGAR Vision - Eg- India as a "First Responder".

  • 8.    Climate Resilience- Security agencies like the Indian Coast Guard play a critical role in responding to oil spills and natural disasters.

Offshore Reserves - (Geological Survey of India)

  • 1.    Heavy Mineral Placers- deposits of ilmenite, rutile, zircon, and monazite

  • 2.    Polymetallic Nodules and Crusts- rich in Cobalt, Nickel, Copper, and Manganese.

  • 3.    Lime Mud and Calcareous Sand- over 1,53,996 million tonnes.

  • 4.    Hydrothermal Sulfides in the Andaman Sea.

  • 5.    Silica-rich construction Sand- Estimated 745 million tons

Maritime Security Challenges in India

STRAIT OF HORMUZ

Q SJRAIT OF MALACCA


  • 1.    The Indian ocean region is ‘New Hotbed’ of security threats - Fareed Zakaria

  • 2.    Maritime Terrorism- Eg- the 26/11 Mumbai attacks

    HF OF ADEN


  • 3.    Geopolitical Competition from China challenging India's role as a "Net Security Provider."


    OSUNDA STRAIT
